title-group.article-title: Prevalence of tuberculosis-related chest radiographic abnormalities among individuals with latent tuberculosis infection in Gulf Cooperation Council countries: A systematic review and meta-analysis

contrib-group.aff: Department of Radiology, College of Medicine, Qassim University, P.O. Box 1162, Qassim, 51452, Saudi Arabia

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Chest radiography is routinely used during latent tuberculosis infection (LTBI) evaluation to exclude active disease.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: PubMed and google scholar were searched for studies reporting chest radiographic abnormalities among individuals with LTBI in Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) countries. Random-effects meta-analyses were conducted to estimate pooled prevalence. Subgroup and meta-regression analyses were performed to explore heterogeneity. Publication bias was assessed funnel plot, Egger's regression and Begg's rank correlation tests. Meta-analysis was performed using OpenMeta Analyst software and Comprehensive meta-analysis version 3.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Twenty included studies yielded a pooled prevalence of chest radiographic abnormalities of 5.8% (95% CI: 2.7–11.2). Between-study variability was high (t2 = 1.742; I2 = 90.93% with Q = 209.569, df = 19, and P < 0.001). By country, the highest and lowest prevalence estimates were 6.1% in Saudi Arabia and 2.4% in Qatar, respectively. By population group, higher prevalence was observed among kidney transplant recipients 37.1%, while prisoners demonstrated very low prevalence 0.1%. Prevalence was 8.0% in hospitals and 0.1% in prisons. Chest X-ray was the predominant imaging modality (5.6%). Meta-regression identified population group and study setting as significant sources of heterogeneity.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Chest radiographic abnormalities occur in a minority of LTBI individuals in GCC countries, with substantial variation across populations and settings.

list-item.p: Prevalence estimates were 6.1% in Saudi Arabia.
Population group and study setting identified as significant sources of heterogeneity.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Tuberculosis (TB) remains one of the top infectious causes of death globally and a major contributor to morbidity across all regions of the world [1,2]. Despite significant advances in diagnosis and treatment, the global burden of TB persists with an estimated 10.7 million new cases and 1.23 million deaths in 2024 [3]. A key impediment to TB elimination is the vast reservoir of latent tuberculosis infection (LTBI), which is estimated to affect approximately one-quarter of the world's population [4]. Individuals with LTBI are asymptomatic and non-infectious, yet remain at risk of progressing to active disease, especially in the presence of immunosuppressive conditions or co-morbidities [5,6]. This reservoir forms the basis for the WHO's emphasis on LTBI detection and preventive treatment as essential components of global TB control strategies [3].

The WHO End TB Strategy, adopted by member states in 2015 and progressively updated to reflect evolving evidence, underscores the importance of integrated, patient-centred care; bold policies and supportive systems; and intensified research and innovation to reduce LTBI-related progression and ultimately eliminate TB [7,8]. Central to this strategy is the recognition that LTBI exists not as a binary state but as a spectrum of infection states, from truly latent, clinically quiescent infection to subclinical disease with radiological evidence of pulmonary involvement [2,6].

Chest radiography, particularly chest X-ray (CXR), remains a cornerstone of TB screening and LTBI evaluation due to its wide availability, relative affordability, and established role in excluding active pulmonary disease [9]. Radiographic abnormalities in the context of LTBI may include fibrotic scars, calcified granulomas, pleural thickening, and nodular opacities [[10], [11], [12]]. These findings are often interpreted as markers of past TB infection or contained granulomatous disease, and have been incorporated into diagnostic algorithms used by TB programs worldwide [13,14]. However, the clinical significance of radiographic abnormalities in LTBI remains a subject of ongoing debate, particularly regarding their predictive value for progression to active TB [10,12].

The prevalence of chest radiographic abnormalities among individuals with LTBI varies widely across studies, driven by differences in host factors such as immunosuppression, repeated exposures, and population characteristics, as well as by variations in screening practices and radiological interpretation [15,16]. Immunocompromised populations, including people living with Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV), transplant recipients, individuals receiving immunosuppressive therapy, and patients with hematologic malignancies, are more likely to exhibit chest radiographic abnormalities and are at significantly higher risk of progression to active TB [17,18].

The Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) countries comprising Saudi Arabia, United Arab Emirates, Qatar, Kuwait, Oman, and Bahrain, represent a distinct epidemiological setting in which TB control must contend with large expatriate populations, high levels of human mobility, and unique occupational and environmental exposures [1]. While the overall incidence of active TB in the GCC is lower than global averages, the demographic structure characterized by a significant portion of migrant workers from high TB burden regions increases the complexity of LTBI epidemiology and the potential for undetected pulmonary sequelae [3,14]. In addition, mass gatherings such as religious pilgrimages create public health imperatives for effective TB surveillance and screening programs.

LTBI screening in the GCC occurs across diverse settings including healthcare facilities, migrant health centers, occupational health programs, university clinics, and community health services. Despite the centrality of CXR in these processes, there is no harmonized, region-wide framework for the interpretation or reporting of radiographic abnormalities, and the prevalence of such abnormalities among LTBI individuals remains poorly characterized. Furthermore, many existing studies are limited by small sample sizes, focus on narrow subpopulations, or lack standardized radiological criteria, which together hinder cross-study comparability and policy translation.

Accordingly, the present study was designed to systematically review and perform a meta-analysis on the prevalence of chest radiographic abnormalities among individuals with LTBI in GCC countries. The objectives of this research were to estimate pooled prevalence across GCC studies, explore sources of heterogeneity through subgroup analyses by country, population group, setting, and radiological modality, and contextualize findings within the WHO End TB Strategy framework. By providing the first regionally focused, methodologically robust synthesis on this topic, this study aims to inform clinical practice, guide policy decisions, and identify priority areas for future research in TB control in the GCC.

sec.title: Study design and protocol
sec.p: For this study, the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analysis (PRISMA 2020) guidelines [19] was used as the checklist (Additional File 1). This study protocol has been registered on the Open Science Framework (OSF) with the identification https://doi.org/10.17605/OSF.IO/XET24.

sec.title: Literature review
sec.p: Abstracts from PROSPERO database and database of abstracts of reviews of effects (DARE) (http://www.library.UCSF.edu) were searched to ensure that no other meta-analysis on the prevalence of chest radiographic abnormalities among Individuals with LTBI in GCC exists or is ongoing. Two electronic biomedical databases (PubMed and Google scholar) were methodically explored for peer-reviewed articles which were in agreement with our inclusion criteria. These databases were searched on January 2026, using the search strategy in Pubmed; ((“latent tuberculosis"[MeSH Terms] OR (“latent"[All Fields] AND "tuberculosis"[All Fields]) OR "latent tuberculosis"[All Fields] OR (“latent"[All Fields] AND "tuberculosis"[All Fields] AND "infection"[All Fields]) OR "latent tuberculosis infection"[All Fields]) AND (“saudi arabia"[MeSH Terms] OR (“saudi"[All Fields] AND "arabia"[All Fields]) OR "saudi arabia"[All Fields])) OR "uae"[All Fields] OR (“oman"[MeSH Terms] OR "oman"[All Fields]) OR (“qatar"[MeSH Terms] OR "qatar"[All Fields] OR "qatar s"[All Fields]) OR (“kuwait"[MeSH Terms] OR "kuwait"[All Fields] OR "kuwait s"[All Fields]) OR (“bahrain"[MeSH Terms] OR "bahrain"[All Fields]) = 86,062 and in Google scholar; (“latent tuberculosis infection" OR LTBI OR "latent TB") AND (“chest radiograph" OR "chest X-ray" OR "radiological abnormalities" OR "chest CT") AND (prevalence OR epidemiology) AND (“Saudi Arabia" OR "United Arab Emirates" OR UAE OR Oman OR Qatar OR Kuwait OR Bahrain) = 1090. In addition, references and titles from included articles were utilized as a supplementary search tool.

sec.title: Inclusion and exclusion criteria for studies
sec.p: Radiographic abnormalities in the context of LTBI is TB related may include fibrotic scars, calcified granulomas, pleural thickening, and nodular opacities etc.

sec.title: Inclusion criteria
sec.p: Cross-sectional studies, cohort studies, screening program reports, or baseline data from interventional studies reporting the prevalence of abnormal chest radiographs or specific radiological findings using CXR and/or CT scan were included. Studies reporting chest radiography findings among LTBI-positive individuals residing in GCC countries (Saudi Arabia, UAE, Qatar, Kuwait, Bahrain, Oman) were also included. Human participants of any age, gender, or ethnicity, general population or high-risk groups (e.g., healthcare workers, migrants, household contacts of TB patients, individuals with HIV, diabetes, or other immunocompromised conditions) were included. Studies employing validated diagnostic methods for LTBI, including: Interferon-gamma release assays (IGRAs: QuantiFERON-TB Gold, T-SPOT.TB), Tuberculin skin test (TST) were included. Studies reporting prevalence or sufficient data to calculate prevalence (number of LTBI positive cases and total sample size) were included. Publications in English were also included.

sec.title: Exclusion criteria
sec.p: Case reports, case series, editorials, letters, commentaries, narrative reviews, conference abstracts, and policy briefs without primary data, animal or laboratory-based studies were excluded. Studies conducted outside GCC, studies including only active TB patients (without LTBI outcomes), Studies relying on non-standardized diagnostic methods without IGRA/TST confirmation and studies with unclear or inconsistent case definitions of LTBI were excluded. Studies not reporting prevalence or data insufficient to calculate prevalence, duplicate publications from the same dataset (the most comprehensive or recent version will be included).

sec.title: Data extraction
sec.p: Study selection and data extraction were conducted using a predefined, standardized protocol developed prior to literature screening. Titles and abstracts were initially screened for eligibility, followed by full-text assessment of potentially relevant articles. To minimize selection bias and extraction errors, each screening and data extraction step was conducted independently at two separate time points by the author, with discrepancies resolved through repeated verification. Data extraction was performed using a piloted standardized extraction form capturing the first author's name, publication year, the country in GCC where study was carried out, characteristics of the population group where chest abnormalities among LTBI individuals were detected, the type of radiological test used and the settings were the sample were collected.

To further minimize potential bias, three independent reviewers were invited through Rayyan (Qatar Computing Research Institute) to verify inclusion and exclusion criteria. These reviewers cross-checked my selections but were not involved in manuscript drafting and preparations.

sec.title: Study quality assessment
sec.p: The Joanna Briggs Institute (JBI) critical appraisal checklist for prevalence data [20] (Additional File 2) was used to evaluate quality of the included studies with repeated evaluation to ensure consistency and accuracy. This iterative verification approach ensured methodological rigor, reproducibility, and data integrity. The JBI checklist was used for item-level critical appraisal. Studies were not assigned an overall score; instead, risk of bias was interpreted across domains. This appraisal checklist contains nine items. Item grades are yes, no, unclear, or not applicable [20]. Studies were included in the analysis if consensus was reached among the reviewers. The quality of the 20 included studies is given in (Additional File 3).

sec.title: Data analysis
sec.p: Prevalence of chest abnormalities among LTBI individuals in GCC was calculated, and subgroup analyses were done according to the country in GCC where study was carried out, characteristics of the population group where chest abnormalities among LTBI individuals were detected, the type of radiological test used and the settings were the sample were collected. Where the prevalence was not reported by a study, they were back-calculated. DerSimonian and Laird method of meta-analysis [21,22] was used to determine the pooled prevalence. The random-effect model was used because heterogeneity was anticipated giving that the studies were carried out in diverse locations and settings.

sec.title: Bias and heterogeneity analysis
sec.p: Within-study biases were evaluated by the country in GCC where study was carried out, characteristics of the population group where chest abnormalities among LTBI individuals were detected, the type of radiological test used and the settings were the sample were collected. Small study effects or bias were examined by funnel plots and trim-and-fill plots. The heterogeneities of study-level estimates were assessed by Cochran's Q test. Non-significant heterogeneity was accepted if the ratio of Q and the degrees of freedom (Q/df) was less than one. The percentage of the variation in prevalence estimates attributable to heterogeneity was measured by the inverse variance index (I2), and I2 values of 25%, 50%, and 75% were considered low, moderate, and high heterogeneity, respectively [22]. The sources of heterogeneity were analyzed using the sensitivity analysis (leave-one-out meta-analysis), subgroup analysis, and meta-regression. Meta-analysis was performed using OpenMeta Analyst software [23] and Comprehensive meta-analysis version 3 [24].

sec.title: Search results and eligible studies
sec.p:
Through database searches, a total of 87152 studies were found, with 35702 duplicates deleted. The titles and abstracts of the remaining 51450 papers were examined, and 51400 were excluded after failing to match any of the inclusion criteria. A total of 50 full-text papers were reviewed for eligibility, with 30 being excluded various reasons (Fig. 1). A total of 20 full-text studies were used for qualitative and quantitative analyses respectively (Fig. 1). The characteristics of the included studies are shown in Table 1.Fig. 1
PRISMA flow diagram for the selection of eligible articles included in the study.Fig. 1Table 1
Characteristics of Studies included in the Final Meta–analysis of the Prevalence of chest radiographic abnormalities among individuals with LTBI in GCC Countries.Table 1
S/NoAuthor, Publication YearCountryPopulation GroupSettingRadiological TestChest Radiographic StatusSample SizeNo PositivePrevalence (%)
1[25]Saudi ArabiaHCWHospitalCXRAbnormal733162.2
2[26]Saudi ArabiaHCWHospitalCXRAbnormal291217.2
3[26]Saudi ArabiaHCWHospitalCTAbnormal291206.9
4[27]Saudi ArabiaPIHDHospitalCXRAbnormal1900.0
5[28]Saudi ArabiaHCWHospitalCXRAbnormal472144.5
6[29]Saudi ArabiaPrisonersPrisonCXRAbnormal74300.0
7[30]Saudi ArabiaHIVHospitalCXRAbnormal200.0
8[31]Saudi ArabiaHCWHPACXRAbnormal10000.0
9[32]Saudi ArabiaStudentsUniversityCXRAbnormal9600.0
10[33]Saudi ArabiaIBDPHospitalCXRAbnormal1600.0
11[34]Saudi ArabiaHDPHospitalCXRAbnormal1231613.0
12[35]Saudi ArabiaKTRHospitalCXRAbnormal702637.1
13[36]Saudi ArabiaHealthy PopulationCommunityCXRAbnormal7800.0
14[37]Saudi ArabiaCancer patientsHospitalCXRAbnormal17423.5
15[38]QatarHCWHospitalCXRAbnormal2000.0
16[39]UAERheumatology patientsHospitalCXRAbnormal6722.99
17[40]UAEPediatric patientsHospitalCXRAbnormal300.0
18[41]OmanHCWHospitalCXRAbnormal15900.0
19[42]OmanImmigrantsHospitalCXRAbnormal23493.8
20[43]OmanHousehold contactsCommunityCXRAbnormal431432.6
HCW = Health care workers; PIHD = Patient with ischemic heart disease; HIV = Human immunodeficiency virus patients; IBDP = Inflammatory bowel disease patient; HDP = Hemodialysis patients; KTR = Kidney transplant recipient; HPA = Hajj pilgrim area; CXR = Chest X-Ray; CT = Computed tomography; UAE = United Arab Emirate.

Overall, the included studies demonstrated high methodological quality, with most studies fulfilling all nine JBI domains. A small number of studies showed limitations in sampling methodology (Q2) and response rate reporting (Q9). However, the majority employed valid diagnostic methods, appropriate statistical analyses, and clearly defined study populations, indicating a generally low risk of bias across included studies.

Studies were not excluded based on sample size to avoid selection bias and loss of relevant data, particularly in underrepresented populations. Random-effects modeling and sensitivity analyses were used to account for variability and assess the influence of small studies.

sec.title: The pooled prevalence of chest abnormalities among LTBI individuals
sec.p:
The pooled prevalence of chest abnormalities among LTBI individuals in GCC countries was calculated to be 5.8% (95% CI; 3.0 – 11.1 %; I2 = 90.93%; P < 0.001) (Fig. 2). To determine the prevalence of chest abnormalities among LTBI individuals in GCC, random-effects meta-analyses were performed utilizing the total sample size and number of positives (effect size, standard error of effect size). Between-study variability was high (t2 = 1.742; heterogeneity I2 = 90.93% with heterogeneity chi-square (Q) = 209.569, degrees of freedom (df) = 19, and P < 0.001). No one study had an effect on the heterogeneity and pooled prevalence of chest abnormalities among LTBI individuals in GCC, as evidenced in the sensitivity analysis's leave-one-out forest plot (Fig. 3). Furthermore, as indicated in the asymmetrical funnel plot, there was publication bias (Fig. 4). In addition to the funnel plot for visual detection of publication bias, the Trim-and-Fill method was applied to include the “missing” studies from the analysis. Because the study was asymmetrical, the Trim-and-Fill method suggested that 5 studies were missing (Fig. 5). Under the random effects model the point estimate and 95% confidence interval for the combined studies is 5.8% (3.0% – 11.1%). Using Trim and Fill the imputed point estimate is 10.1% (5.5% - 18.0%). The Egger's regression t = 1.3533 (intercept = −1.65349; 95% CI; (−4.22 – 0.91; P = 0.0964) and the Begg's rank correlation test (P = 0.2478) did not confirm publication bias.Fig. 2
Forest plot showing the pooled prevalence of chest abnormalities among LTBI individuals in GCC Countries.Fig. 2Fig. 3
Leave-one-out Forest plot showing the pooled prevalence of chest abnormalities among LTBI individuals in GCC Countries.Fig. 3Fig. 4
Funnel plot showing publication bias (no symmetry) in studies reporting the prevalence of chest abnormalities among LTBI individuals in GCC Countries.Fig. 4Fig. 5
Funnel plot showing 5 added studies (in red) on the right in the Trim-and- Fill method reporting the prevalence of chest abnormalities among LTBI individuals in GCC Countries.Fig. 5

sec.title: Subgroup meta-analysis
sec.p: Subgroup analyses were performed according to the country in GCC where study was carried out, characteristics of the population group where chest abnormalities among LTBI individuals were detected, the type of radiological test used and the settings were the sample were collected to find the likely sources of variability among studies, as high heterogeneity was detected (I2 = 90.93%).

sec.title: Country-level prevalence of chest abnormalities among LTBI individuals in GCC
sec.p:
Country-specific subgroup analysis showed marked variation in the prevalence of Chest abnormalities among LTBI individuals across GCC countries. Saudi Arabia, contributing the largest number of studies (n = 14), had a pooled prevalence of 6.1% (95% CI: 2.7–13.0) with substantial heterogeneity (I2 = 92.3%, P < 0.001). Oman showed a prevalence of 5.2% (95% CI: 0.6–34.4) and similarly high heterogeneity (I2 = 94.3%, P < 0.001). In contrast, the UAE demonstrated a lower prevalence of 3.9% (95% CI: 1.1–12.7) with no observed heterogeneity (I2 = 0%). Qatar was represented by a single study, reporting a prevalence of 2.4% (95% CI: 0.1–28.7), precluding heterogeneity assessment (Table 2). No study was reported in Kuwait and Bahrain. The forest plot is shown in Fig. 6 and the map shown in Fig. 7.Table 2
Country – level subgroup analyses of chest abnormalities among LTBI individuals in GCC countries.Table 2
CoutryNumber of StudiesPrevalence (%)95% CII2 (%)QHeterogeneity Test
DFP
Saudi Arabia146.12.7 – 13.092.32169.32513<0.001
Qatar12.40.1 – 28.7----
UAE23.91.1 – 12.700.84210.359
Oman35.20.6 – 34.494.3235.1852<0.001
UAE = United Arab Emirate.Fig. 6
Forest plot showing Country – level prevalence of chest abnormalities among LTBI individuals in GCC.Fig. 6Fig. 7
Map of GCC Countries showing number of studies and country-specific prevalence of chest Abnormalities among LTBI individuals.Fig. 7

sec.title: Population group prevalence of chest abnormalities among LTBI individuals in GCC countries
sec.p:
Population-level subgroup analysis demonstrated substantial variability in the prevalence of chest abnormalities among individuals with LTBI across different risk groups in the GCC countries. Health care workers (HCWs), the most frequently studied group (n = 7), showed a pooled prevalence of 4.7% (95% CI: 1.5–13.5) with considerable heterogeneity (I2 = 93.9%, P < 0.001), indicating marked between-study variability (Table 3). Higher prevalence were observed among immunocompromised and high-risk populations, including kidney transplant recipients (37.1%; 95% CI: 26.7–49.0), household contacts (32.6%; 95% CI: 20.3–47.7), cancer patients (23.5%; 95% CI: 9.1–48.6), and HIV-infected individuals (16.7%; 95% CI: 1.0–80.6). Moderate prevalence were reported in hemodialysis patients (13.0%) and pediatric populations (12.5%), although confidence intervals were wide. In contrast, low prevalence were noted among prisoners, students, healthy populations, and immigrants (Table 3). Most subgroups were represented by single studies, precluding heterogeneity assessment and warranting cautious interpretation. The forest plot is shown in Fig. 8.Table 3
Population group – Level Subgroup Analyses of the Prevalence of Chest Abnormalities among LTBI Individuals in GCC Countries.Table 3
Population GroupNumber of StudiesPrevalence (%)95% CII2 (%)QHeterogeneity Test
DFP
HCW74.71.5 – 13.593.8597.4896<0.001
PIHD12.50.2 – 29.8----
Prisoners10.10.0 – 1.1----
HIV116.71.0 – 80.6----
Students10.50.0 – 7.7----
IBDP12.90.2 – 33.6----
HDP113.08.1 – 20.2----
KTR137.126.7 – 49.0----
Healthy population10.60.0 – 9.3----
Cancer patients123.59.1 – 48.6----
Rheumatology patients13.00.7 – 11.2----
Paediatric patients112.50.7 – 73.4----
Immigrants13.82.0 – 7.2----
Household contacts132.620.3 – 47.7----
HCW = Health care workers; PIHD = Patient with ischemic heart disease; HIV = Human immunodeficiency virus patients; IBDP = Inflammatory bowel disease patient; HDP = Hemodialysis patients; KTR = Kidney transplant recipient.Fig. 8
Forest plot showing Population group – level prevalence of chest abnormalities among LTBI individuals in GCC Countries.Fig. 8

sec.title: Radiological tests prevalence of chest abnormalities among LTBI individuals in GCC countries
sec.p:
Radiological test–level subgroup analysis indicated that CXR was the predominant modality used to detect chest abnormalities among LTBI individuals in the GCC countries, contributing 19 studies (Table 4). The pooled prevalence of chest abnormalities detected by CXR was 5.6% (95% CI: 2.7–11.2), with substantial heterogeneity across studies (I2 = 91.2%, P < 0.001), suggesting considerable variability in study populations and imaging interpretation. Only one study used CT, reporting a prevalence of 6.9% (95% CI: 4.5–10.4) (Table 4). Due to the single CT-based study, heterogeneity could not be assessed. The forest plot is shown in Fig. 9.Table 4
Radiological test – level subgroup analyses of the prevalence of chest abnormalities among LTBI individuals in GCC countries.Table 4
Diagnostic TestsNumber of StudiesPrevalence (%)95% CII2 (%)QHeterogeneity Test
DFP
CXR195.62.7 – 11.291.19204.31918<0.001
CT Scan16.94.5 – 10.4----
CXR = Chest X-Ray; CT = Computed tomography.Fig. 9
Forest plot showing the prevalence of chest abnormalities among LTBI Individuals in GCC Countries in relation to radiological test used.Fig. 9

sec.title: Prevalence of chest abnormalities among LTBI individuals in GCC countries according to different settings
sec.p:
Setting-level analysis of LTBI prevalence in GCC countries revealed notable variation across study environments. Hospital-based studies, which constituted the majority (n = 15), reported a pooled LTBI prevalence of 8.0% (95% CI: 4.0–15.4), accompanied by substantial heterogeneity (I2 = 91.4%, P < 0.001), indicating marked differences in study populations and diagnostic approaches (Table 5). Community-based studies (n = 2) showed a prevalence of 6.5% (95% CI: 0.1–82.5) with high heterogeneity (I2 = 88.7%, P = 0.003), reflecting wide uncertainty due to limited data. In contrast, very low prevalence estimates were observed in prison settings (0.1%), Hajj pilgrim areas (0.5%), and university settings (0.5%), each represented by a single study (Table 5) and their heterogeneity could not be assessed. The forest plot is shown in Fig. 10.Table 5
Settings – level prevalence of chest abnormalities among LTBI individuals in GCC countries.Table 5
SettingsNumber of StudiesPrevalence (%)95% CII2 (%)QHeterogeneity Test
DFP
Hospital158.04.0 – 15.491.43163.33414<0.001
Prison10.10.0 – 1.1----
HPA10.50.0 – 7.4----
University10.50.0 – 7.7----
Community26.50.1 – 82.588.698.84110.003
HPA = Hajj pilgrim area.Fig. 10
Forest plot showing the prevalence of chest abnormalities among LTBI Individuals in GCC Countries in relation to various settings.Fig. 10

sec.title: Meta-regression
sec.p: Meta regression were done using methods of moments as the computational option. The meta-regression explored sources of heterogeneity in the prevalence of LTBI across multiple moderators, including radiological test, country, population group, and study setting. The findings reveal that certain covariates significantly account for variability in prevalence estimates, while others do not.

The random-effects meta-regression analyses examined whether country, population group, study setting, and radiological modality explained variability in the prevalence of chest abnormalities among LTBI individuals in GCC. Country-level analysis showed no statistically significant differences between Oman, Saudi Arabia, and the UAE, with all coefficients non-significant (P > 0.60) and minimal residual heterogeneity (Q = 0.23, P = 0.97), indicating no country effect on prevalence (Fig. 11).Fig. 11
A Meta Regression Analysis in relation to the Prevalence of chest abnormalities among LTBI Individuals in GCC Countries.Fig. 11

Population-group analysis similarly demonstrated no significant associations for most subgroups, including healthcare workers, HIV patients, household contacts, immigrants, kidney transplant recipients, and patients with chronic diseases. However, prisoners showed a significantly lower logit event rate (coefficient = −6.13, P = 0.012), suggesting a significantly lower prevalence of chest abnormalities compared with the reference group. Overall heterogeneity across population groups was not statistically significant (Q = 16.10, P = 0.24) (Fig. 11).

Setting-level analysis revealed a significant overall subgroup effect (Q = 10.29, P = 0.036). Specifically, prison settings were associated with a significantly lower prevalence (coefficient = −5.19, P = 0.017), while hospital, university, and Hajj pilgrim area settings were not significantly different from the reference.
Radiological modality did not significantly influence prevalence estimates, as CXR use was not associated with differences in logit event rates (P = 0.88) (Fig. 11).

Overall, these findings suggest that population and setting characteristics, particularly prison populations and settings rather than country or imaging modality, contribute to observed variability in prevalence estimates.
The scatter plot for the Meta regression is shown in Additional File 4–7.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This systematic review and meta-analysis provide the first comprehensive synthesis of the prevalence of chest radiographic abnormalities among individuals with LTBI in GCC countries. The findings demonstrate that while the overall prevalence of radiographic abnormalities among LTBI individuals is relatively low, there is substantial heterogeneity across populations, settings, and study designs. This variability reflects both epidemiological diversity and differences in screening practices, diagnostic thresholds, and population risk profiles across the region. Importantly, the results offer critical insights for TB prevention policies and implementation strategies in GCC countries, particularly when aligned with the WHO End TB Strategy.

The pooled prevalence of chest radiographic abnormalities among LTBI individuals suggests that a non-negligible proportion of infected persons exhibit residual or subclinical pulmonary changes despite the absence of active disease. These abnormalities likely represent healed TB lesions, fibrotic changes, or calcifications resulting from prior exposure or contained infection, which are well-described manifestations within the TB disease spectrum [2,10,12].

However, the consistently high heterogeneity observed across analyses indicates that LTBI is not a uniform biological or clinical state but rather a continuum influenced by host immunity, exposure intensity, and environmental context. Such heterogeneity is consistent with contemporary understanding of TB pathogenesis, which challenges the binary distinction between latent and active disease [5]. In this context, chest radiographic abnormalities may reflect intermediate states of infection that have implications for disease progression risk and preventive therapy decisions.

Country-level subgroup and meta-regression analyses did not demonstrate statistically significant differences in prevalence across GCC countries. This suggests a broadly comparable epidemiological pattern of LTBI-related radiographic abnormalities across the region. Shared characteristics such as reliance on expatriate labor from TB-endemic countries, similar healthcare infrastructure, and comparable TB control strategies may partly explain this homogeneity [8].

However, the absence of significant country effects should be interpreted cautiously. Several GCC countries were represented by few studies, limiting statistical power and masking potential within-country variation. These findings highlight the need for more nationally representative data and underscore the importance of strengthening TB surveillance and reporting systems at both national and regional levels.

Population-level subgroup analyses revealed marked variation in prevalence estimates, reinforcing the role of host-related factors in shaping radiographic outcomes among LTBI individuals. Higher prevalence estimates were observed among immunocompromised and clinically vulnerable groups, including kidney transplant recipients, cancer patients, people living with HIV, and household contacts. These findings are biologically plausible, as impaired cellular immunity increases susceptibility to both progression and residual lung pathology following TB exposure [44].

Meta-regression further identified prisoners as having a significantly lower prevalence of chest abnormalities compared with reference populations. While counterintuitive given the recognized TB risk in correctional settings, this finding may reflect younger age distributions, selection bias toward healthier individuals, or systematic entry screening that excludes those with prior pulmonary disease [29,45]. It may also indicate under-detection related to limited radiological evaluation or conservative interpretation thresholds in prison health systems.
These findings underscore the importance of risk stratification in LTBI screening and management, rather than uniform approaches applied across heterogeneous populations.

Setting-level meta-regression demonstrated that prison settings were significantly associated with lower prevalence estimates, while hospital, university, and Hajj pilgrim area settings were not significantly different from reference categories. Hospital-based studies tended to report higher prevalence estimates, likely reflecting referral bias and higher comorbidity burdens among screened populations.

The diversity of settings represented in this review illustrates how contextual factors influence observed prevalence and reinforces the need to interpret LTBI screening data within the operational realities of each setting. These findings are particularly relevant in GCC countries, where LTBI screening is conducted across diverse environments, including healthcare facilities, migrant health centers, educational institutions, and mass gathering contexts.

CXR was the predominant imaging modality used across studies, with only one study employing CT. Meta-regression did not demonstrate a significant effect of imaging modality on prevalence estimates. While CT is more sensitive for detecting subtle parenchymal changes, its limited use reflects cost, radiation exposure, and feasibility constraints in large-scale screening programs Creswell et al., 2023; [9].

The lack of a modality effect suggests that most abnormalities detected in LTBI populations are sufficiently overt to be identified on standard chest radiographs. This supports the continued use of CXR as the primary imaging tool in LTBI evaluation, particularly in resource-conscious screening strategies.
The findings of this review align closely with the three pillars of the WHO End TB Strategy and offer actionable guidance for policy and practice in GCC countries [46,47].

Further, the findings of this meta-analysis have important implications for TB prevention policies and LTBI management strategies in GCC countries. Although the overall prevalence of chest radiographic abnormalities among individuals with LTBI is relatively low, the consistent detection of abnormalities, particularly among high-risk populations and in hospital-based settings, highlights the need for more targeted, risk-stratified implementation approaches rather than uniform screening strategies.

Current LTBI screening policies across the GCC primarily emphasize immunological testing (TST or IGRA), with chest radiography often used to exclude active disease. The present findings support maintaining CXR as a core component of LTBI evaluation but suggest that its use should be prioritized for high-risk populations, including transplant candidates, oncology patients, people living with HIV, close household contacts, and healthcare workers. For these groups, the presence of radiographic abnormalities may reflect prior or subclinical pulmonary involvement and could inform decisions regarding preventive therapy initiation, regimen selection, and follow-up intensity [33].

Substantial heterogeneity across studies underscores the absence of standardized definitions and reporting frameworks for chest radiographic abnormalities in LTBI. Policymakers should prioritize the development and adoption of regional radiological reporting standards, aligned with WHO guidance and adapted to the GCC context. Standardized descriptors, distinguishing healed TB lesions, fibrotic changes, calcifications, and nonspecific abnormalities, would improve diagnostic consistency, enhance surveillance quality, and facilitate cross-country comparability.

Given the demographic profile of GCC countries, where migrant workers constitute a substantial proportion of the population, the findings have direct relevance for pre-employment, post-arrival, and periodic health screening policies. While mass radiological screening of all migrants may not be cost-effective, targeted CXR evaluation for migrants originating from high TB-burden countries or entering high-risk occupations (e.g., healthcare, domestic work) could be justified.

Similarly, occupational health policies for healthcare workers should integrate periodic LTBI screening with radiological assessment for those with immunosuppression, prolonged exposure, or prior abnormal findings. These measures would align TB prevention efforts with broader workforce health and safety frameworks.

The relatively low prevalence of radiographic abnormalities among LTBI individuals suggests that universal advanced imaging like CT is unlikely to be cost-effective in routine screening. Policymakers should therefore reserve CT imaging for selected high-risk individuals with abnormal or equivocal CXR findings or those undergoing immunosuppressive therapy. This tiered approach balances diagnostic sensitivity with cost containment and radiation safety considerations.

Several limitations should be acknowledged. First, substantial heterogeneity persisted across pooled and subgroup analyses, reflecting variability in study design, populations, and diagnostic practices and potential publication bias. Second is the limited database search of PubMed and google scholar. Third, many subgroups were represented by single studies, limiting the robustness and generalizability of estimates. Forth, most studies relied on CXR, which has limited sensitivity for detecting subtle or inactive TB-related changes. Fifth, inconsistent reporting of age, comorbidities, prior TB exposure, and LTBI diagnostic methods limited adjustment for confounding. Finally, the cross-sectional nature of included studies precludes causal inference regarding the relationship between radiographic abnormalities and progression to active TB.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Despite these limitations, this study provides the most comprehensive synthesis to date of chest radiographic abnormalities among LTBI individuals in GCC countries. The findings support a transition toward risk-based, standardized, and policy-aligned LTBI screening strategies, grounded in the WHO End TB Strategy. Further studies are needed to standardize radiological definitions of TB-related abnormalities, conduct large-scale prospective and multicenter studies across underrepresented GCC countries, and incorporate advanced imaging modalities such as CT. Research focusing on high-risk populations, integration of immunological and radiological diagnostics, and longitudinal assessment of progression to active TB is essential. Additionally, harmonized methodologies and consideration of population mobility and One Health dynamics are required to reduce heterogeneity and improve the generalizability of findings.

sec.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
sec.p: Not applicable.

sec.title: Clinical trial number
sec.p: Not applicable.

sec.title: Availability of data and materials
sec.p: Dataset used and/or analyzed during the correct study are included in the manuscript and in Supplementary files.

sec.title: Declaration of use of AI
sec.p: During the preparation of this manuscript/study, the author used ChatGPT and Quilbot for the purposes of improving readability and language. The author have reviewed and edited the output and take full responsibility for the content of this publication.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: Funding was provided by Deanship of Graduate Studies and Scientific Research at 10.13039/501100007414Qassim University for financial support (QU-APC-2026).

sec.title: Declaration of competing interest
sec.p: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ack.title: Acknowledgment
ack.p: The Researchers would like to thank the Deanship of Graduate Studies and Scientific Research at 10.13039/501100007414Qassim University for financial support (QU-APC-2026). The author would also acknowledge the contributions of the three blind, independent reviewers invited through Rayyan for their assistance in verifying study selection. Their support improved the strength of the review process; however, they are not listed as co-authors in accordance with authorship criteria.
